coredata.dc:title: Bisphenol A/Bisphenol F mineralization in the presence of self-decorated carbon-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites under multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sheik Moideen Thaha, S.K.
Sathishkumar, Panneerselvam
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we attempted to synthesize a novel, highly efficient, surface-modified nanocatalyst supported by emerging 2D materials such as MXene (Ti3C2). A Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 ternary Z-scheme nanostructure tethered with self-decorated Carbon Quantum Dots (C-QDs) was successfully synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The study presents a pioneering attempt of multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis (MFASP) for the catalytic degradation of bisphenols. The surface morphology and various contours of pristine and nanocomposites were confirmed through various analytical techniques. The decorated C-QDs, ranging from 6 to 8 nm in size were uniformly distributed across the surface of the resulting nanocomposites. The sonophotocatalytic efficiency for BPA degradation was evaluated under various operational conditions, including catalytic dosage, combinational frequencies, and nanocatalyst variations. Notably, under 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonophotocatalytic conditions, the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 catalyst achieved 80 % degradation of BPA within 90 min (k = 0.0180 min−1). The comparison studies revealed that the order BPA elimination at MFASP was BTG > BGC > BOC > TiO2 > BMC > g-C3N4 > Ti2C3. The enhanced production of hydroxyl radicals in multi-frequency conditions was confirmed via terephthalic acid dosimetry method. Additionally, an attempt was conducted under optimized MFASP conditions for the simultaneous removal of BPA and BPF. Ultimately, more than 16 degradation intermediates proceeded through 4 different pathways (ring rupture, decarboxylation, dehydroxylation, bis-benzene ring cleavage) were identified by HRMS/QToFMS analysis, and a plausible decontamination mechanism was proposed.

head.ce:title: Bisphenol A/Bisphenol F mineralization in the presence of self-decorated carbon-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites under multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Vellore 632014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Vellore 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we attempted to synthesize a novel, highly efficient, surface-modified nanocatalyst supported by emerging 2D materials such as MXene (Ti3C2). A Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 ternary Z-scheme nanostructure tethered with self-decorated Carbon Quantum Dots (C-QDs) was successfully synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. The study presents a pioneering attempt of multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis (MFASP) for the catalytic degradation of bisphenols. The surface morphology and various contours of pristine and nanocomposites were confirmed through various analytical techniques. The decorated C-QDs, ranging from 6 to 8 nm in size were uniformly distributed across the surface of the resulting nanocomposites. The sonophotocatalytic efficiency for BPA degradation was evaluated under various operational conditions, including catalytic dosage, combinational frequencies, and nanocatalyst variations. Notably, under 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonophotocatalytic conditions, the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 catalyst achieved 80 % degradation of BPA within 90 min (k = 0.0180 min−1). The comparison studies revealed that the order BPA elimination at MFASP was BTG > BGC > BOC > TiO2 > BMC > g-C3N4 > Ti2C3. The enhanced production of hydroxyl radicals in multi-frequency conditions was confirmed via terephthalic acid dosimetry method. Additionally, an attempt was conducted under optimized MFASP conditions for the simultaneous removal of BPA and BPF. Ultimately, more than 16 degradation intermediates proceeded through 4 different pathways (ring rupture, decarboxylation, dehydroxylation, bis-benzene ring cleavage) were identified by HRMS/QToFMS analysis, and a plausible decontamination mechanism was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In this modern era, leaching of lethal toxins in omnipresent water resources has clutched environmental equivalence of human and other form of life [1,2]. Among all the contaminants present in the total environment, endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) such as Bisphenols (BPs) are paramount substances in proliferating various health risks in humans and other living organisms, even at trace levels [3]. Over prolonged period, BPs persistence transforms the signalling pathways of endocrine system. Hence, the impact of transformed endocrine signals on human health is highly prone to severe diseases such as neurological system dysfunctions, miscarriage, infertility and cancers [4]. Due to their environmental risks and impacts, numerous countries have regulated the utilisation of BPs in domestic and commercial applications. Thus, considering the noxious intricacy of BPs, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) have limited the use of Bisphenol A (BPA) to 0.5 ng/kg in all food packages [5]. The removal of BPA with zero secondary pollution was the interest of many research communities. Since BPA and its derivatives leached in baby foods from polycarbonate bottles have demonstrated the urgency of a strategic and dynamic solution for the efficient detoxification of BPs [6,7].

Sonocatalytic decontamination of environmental pollutants is regarded as a sustainable solution for the future, offering zero secondary pollution and an environmental friendly process. Moreover, combining photocatalysis with ultrasound energy presents various advantages, including enhanced mass transfer, surface cleaning, internal turbulence, and improved catalytic efficiency [8]. Various photocatalytic reactor models have been demonstrated in numerous studies; however, the combined technologies have proven to overcome many of the shortcomings [9]. In this context, acoustic waves generated by multi-frequency ultrasound have emerged and are employed in numerous fields [10–12]. Applying two or more ultrasonic frequencies simultaneously accelerates the nucleation and cavitational events, leading to enhanced quantum efficiency of overall processes. Various attempts were made in the literature to explore the suitable nanocatalysts for sonocatalytic degradation under monofrequency conditions. The extended utilisation of multifrequency in the sonophotocatalytic reaction expected to enhance the rate of degradation and mineralization processes.

Contemporary developments of nanoscience and nanotechnology necessitates, the nanocatalysts with high oxidative properties, non-toxicity and low cost, which gained the heightened attention in the field of environmental remediation [13–15]. Novel surface-engineered nanocatalysts, carbon quantum dots-coated, ultra-small nanoparticles, COF-derived heterojunctions and plasma treated metal oxides, have become the focal points of recent investigation [16–22]. Bismuth-based semiconductor nanocatalysts, such as Bi2O3, exhibits promising photocatalytic efficiency. Various combinations of Bi2O3 including BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I), BiVO4, BiWO4, and Bi2O2CO3, have been explored in the literature [23–26]. However, the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs limits their quantum efficiency. To augment the quantum efficiency further, co-catalysts like r-GO, Co3O4, FeVO4, and metals (Ag, Au, Pt) have been incorporated to form heterojunctions [27–30]. Among the Aurivillius/Sillén group materials (Bi2O2)2+, Bi2O2CO3 having wide range of optical bandgap (2.7 to 3.4 eV) which enables the enhanced visible light absorption characteristics, moreover, the VB potential at 3.7 eV enriches the generation of radicals during oxidation processes [31]. Additionally, CO3 2) interleaved in the lattice layers of (Bi2O2)2+ prospects large internal electric fields and causes asymmetric polarization effects, due to which it has garnered significant attention in materials chemistry [32,33].

In addition, MXene’s 2D structure gained the attention from the environmental decontamination perspective since it acts as an electron reservoir, trapping electrons, conductive properties for interfacial charge transfer and providing active sites for acoustic nucleation [34]. MXene based research has exponentially increased due to their versatile properties, positioning them as strong candidates to replace graphite-based materials [35,36]. Similarly, g-C3N4, a metal-free, 2D polymeric material with a narrow bandgap, offers excellent visible light absorption. Its eco-friendliness, low cost, and versatile optical properties make it as a valuable p-type semiconductor in heterojunctions. Although progress has been made with binary combinations of MXene and Bi2O2CO3 [37,38], constructing an intentional Z-scheme using g-C3N4 and MXene with Bi2O2CO3 is still unexplored.
In this work, we have attempted to

(i) synthesize a novel ternary, C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite from a simple hydrothermal processes, targeting BPA degradation through multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis,

(ii) nanocomposite’s surface, crystalline structure, optical, and elemental properties were characterized using advanced analytical techniques such as HR-TEM, XRD, DRS, photoluminescence, and XPS, (iii) optimized the influence of catalytic dosage, combinational ultrasonic energies (20 kHz, 40 kHz, and 80 kHz), and catalyst variations, including synergistic evaluation on the sonophotocatalytic degradation of BPA, BPF and their structural analogies (iv) degradation intermediates and pathways of BPs was investigated, finally, the overall mechanistic pathway of multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis assisted BPs was portrayed.

Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), melamine (C3H6N6), Nitric acid (HNO3), Terephthalic acid (C8H6O4), Bisphenol A ((CH3)2C(C6H4OH)2), Bisphenol F (CH2(C6H4OH)2) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Multilayer MXene Titanium carbide (99 %) was procured from Nanoshel UK Ltd. All the chemicals were used as received, without any further purification, unless otherwise specified. All the solutions were prepared using the deionised (DI) water.

Graphite like carbon nitride nanoparticles (g-C3N4) was synthesized via one-pot template-free thermal condensation method. In a typical synthesis process, 5g of melamine was transferred into a closed silica crucible. The crucible was covered with multiple layers of aluminium foil tightly to trap the N2 gas. Then the melamine was kept in a muffle furnace and heated at 550°C with heating ramp of 3°C min−1 and kept for 6 h later allowed to reach the room temperature. The resulting pale-yellow powder samples were collected and washed with ethanol for 4 times subsequently dried at 90°C for 12 h.

Hydrothermal method was adopted to prepare C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4. Typically, 2.16 g of bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate was mixed with 30 mL of DI water and kept under magnetic stirring for 1 hour (Solution A). In another beaker, 3.68 g of Na2CO3 was mixed with 50 mL of DI water and kept under magnetic stirring for 1 hour (Solution B). To the solution A, few drops of 1 M HNO3 were added to maintain the pH at 5.5 – 6. Later, Solution B was added drop by drop to solution A with continuous stirring (550 rpm for 30 mins) until milky white precipitate is formed. A calculated amount of MXene and g-C3N4 of wt 1 % (9.8 mg and 5.4 mg) was added to the white slurry and ultrasonicated for 30 min (35 kHz, Cole-Parmer), the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 160°C for 24 hours. After attaining room temperature, the solution was filtered using 0.45 µm nylon membrane. Finally, the gathered samples were dried at 90°C for 12 hours to obtain 1% doped Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 (BTG). A similar procedure was adopted to prepare pristine Bi2O2CO3 (BOC), Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4 (BGC), and Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 (BMC).

The morphological properties, particle distribution, and crystalline nature of the as-prepared nanocomposites were analysed using a FEI –TECNAI TEM instrument (G2 −20 TWIN) with field emission gun operating at 200 kV. The crystallographic phases of the nanocatalyst were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) equipped with 2.2 KW Cu anode and scan range varied from 10 − 90°. Additionally, the crystallite size of the nanocomposites were calculated using Debye-Scherrer equation. UV–Vis NIR diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (JASCO, V-670) equipped with an integrating sphere and barium sulphate as reflectance standard. XPS analyses were performed with a ULVAC-PHI VersaProbe 4 with monochromatic Al-Kα for X-ray source, and the spectra of Bi4f, O1s, C1s, N1s, and Ti2p valence bands were recorded. Fourier transform Infrared spectra (FTIR) of the samples were recorded in the KBr pellet mode of frequency range of 400–4000 cm-1 using Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific with resolution 8 cm−1. The photoluminescence analyses of the powder samples were excited at 305 nm and terephthalate dosimetry was conducted to evaluate the generation of reactive oxygen species using fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI, F-7000). Zeta potential of the as prepared nanocomposites was analysed using DLS instrument – Horiba Scientific (SZ-100). The BPs aliquot samples were analysed using High-performance liquid chromatography (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan), fitted with a CBM-20A system, binary LC-20AP pump and SPD-M20A Photo Diode Array (PDA) detector equipped with C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm). The binary gradient of acetonitrile, and water in a ratio of 50:50 served as mobile phase and run in an isocratic mode with flow rate of 1 mL min−1. The sonophotocatalytic degradation products were identified using High-Resolution-Mass-Spectroscopy (HRMS) with WATERS-XEVO G2-XS-QTof instrument using ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 Column (180 Å, 1.7 μm, 2.1 mm x 150 mm).

The schematic representation of fabricated sonophotocatalytic reactor was depicted in Fig. 1 a. A hexagonal shaped stainless-steel bath with working volume 2L possessing variable ultrasound frequencies was designed (Labsonics, India). Three distinct frequencies can be irradiated: 20 kHz (±3 kHz), 40 kHz, and 80 kHz applied either separately or in combined irradiation modes. Each ultrasound frequency mode has a maximum of input power of 200 W. Two plate-type transducers are mounted on opposite sides of the hexagonal faces. Additionally, an external overhead homogenizer was used, which allows for controllable RPM and features three propeller blades with a length of 1.5 cm. An external water chiller was attached to maintain the constant temperature inside the ultrasonic bath. A top-illuminated photocatalytic reactor model was adopted, hexagonal top was mounted with three detachable 14 W blue LED lamps in triangular shape (457 nm, Philips) as a visible light source (Fig. 1a & b).

The multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted with 20 kHz (± 3 kHz), 40 kHz and 80 kHz of ultrasound irradiation along with top illuminated blue LED (14 W) working at same time. A baseline condition of 20 mg/L-1 of BPA concentration, initial pH of 7, US frequency of 20 kHz and BTG concentration of 1.0 g/L-1 was fixed to evaluate degradation. The sonophotocatalytic microenvironment was homogenized using an overhead homogenizer (500 rpm). Before each experiment, the sonophotocatalytic slurry was stirred for 45 min in the dark condition (without ultrasound and light irradiation) to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the sonophotocatalyst and BPs. The equilibrated samples were irradiated with light and ultrasound energy as per experimental parameter (mono frequency/dual frequency/triple frequency). Samples were collected at regular time intervals and filtered through 0.45 µm PVDF syringe filter for further HPLC and HRMS analyses. The batch experiments of photocatalytic and sonocatalytic degradation were also conducted with a similar experimental condition in presence and absence of light energy and ultrasound waves.

The morphology and nanoarchitecture of the synthesised nanocomposites were characterised by high-resolution transmission electron micrographs (HRTEM). Fig. 2 a and b represents the carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) decorated on the surface of Bi2O2CO3 supported g-C3N4/Ti3C2 (BTG). From the TEM micrograph it can be understood that the hydrothermal synthetic approach led to produce ∼6 nm sized C-QDs (insert of Fig. 2b), thus confirms the formation of C-QDs at the surface of BTG nanocomposites. Nevertheless, few distended C-QDs were identified at the nanocomposites edges/lattice boundaries due to the open-air exposure and agglomeration by the electron beam. Further, it could be evidenced from TEM micrographs (Fig. 2), the multi-layered Ti3C2 is successfully impregnated into the Bi2O2CO3 nanostructure along with the g-C3N4 which is apparently present as nanorods [39–42]. In addition to that, TEM micrographs of pristine g-C3N4 confirms the formation of nanorods like architecture (Fig. S1a). However the length and diameter of the g-C3N4 nanorods were significantly altered during the preparation of BTG which indicates the effective interaction of p-type g-C3N4 in the resulting nanocomposites. It is evidenced from Fig. S1b that C-QDs were resulted from the hydrothermal synthesis of n-type Bi2O2CO3 nanocatalysts. The successive impregnation of Ti3C2 nanolayers and mass decoration of C-QDs on the surface of BTG was shown in Fig. 2(c & d). The polycrystalline nature of the synthesised BTG nanocomposites was confirmed from the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (insert of Fig. 2d).

Subsequently, the lattice boundaries of ternary nanocomposites and pristine Bi2O2CO3 nanocomposites were depicted in Fig. 3 a & b respectively. The calculated inter-planar distance of the ternary nanostructures are 0.29 nm and 0.35 nm which are indexed to the (1 0 3) crystal plane of Bi2O2CO3 and (0 0 6) crystal plane of Ti3C2 [43,44]. The calculated d-spacing distances of g-C3N4 lattice is 0.31 nm, nonetheless g-C3N4 serves as the underlying substrate as a result of it only few lattice fringes were observed from the surface based TEM analysis. Moreover, the formation of heterojunction between the Bi2O2CO3, MXene and g-C3N4 is substantiated by the dissolution of crystal boundaries as indicated in Fig. 3a. It can be concluded that the formation of C-QDs on the surface of pristine Bi2O2CO3 resulting from the hydrothermal synthesis was evidenced from Fig. 3c also supported by Fig. S1b. Furthermore, Fig. 3d displays defect-less boundary formation and internally stacked layers of pristine Bi2O2CO3. The SAED patterns reveals the polycrystalline nature of BTG which resulted due to the presence of C-QDs, g-C3N4 and MXene counterparts, whereas the pristine Bi2O2CO3 displayed semi-crystalline nature (insert of Fig. 3d). The EDX analysis (Fig. S2) confirms the elemental presence of Bi, Ti, C, N and O in the synthesised nanocomposites and the composition of the elements were provided in the Table S1.

The XRD patterns of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 and the pristine Bi2O2CO3 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 4 a. All the diffraction peaks of pristine Bi2O2CO3 can be indexed to the tetragonal crystal structure (space group: I4/mmm (139) JCPDS Card No: 00–025-1464). No impurity peaks were identified, thus confirming the purity of the synthesized Bi2O2CO3 nanocomposites. Furthermore, the crystal structure of Bi2O2CO3 was not altered after doping of g-C3N4/Ti3C2 (2D nanostructures) which was confirmed from Fig. 4a. In addition, the diffractograms pertaining to the C-QDs were not excitingly appeared in the XRD pattern since the diffractograms belonging to the Bi2O2CO3 (JCPDS Card No: 00–025-1464) and C-QDs could be matched at 2θ values of 29.4, 42.1, 52.3 in the resulting nanocomposites. The average crystallite size of the nanocomposites were calculated by Debye-Scherer relation at the high intense peak for pristine and doped nanocomposites. The average crystallite size of BTG nanocomposites was ∼ 22–26 nm. No characteristic peaks of C-QDs, g-C3N4 and MXene have been recognised from the ternary nanocomposite’s XRD pattern, which is ascribed to the dominant crystallinity nature of Bi2O2CO3, lower doping amount of g-C3N4/Ti3C2 and well dispersion of C-QDs throughout the nanocomposites surface [45–48]. Upon careful investigation of pristine Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites, we observed the prominent peaks of (1 0 3) and (1 1 0) were slightly shifted to the lower diffraction angle (0.14°) which is evidenced due to the impregnation of crystal lattices by g-C3N4 and Ti3C2. Subsequently, the characteristic peaks of pure g-C3N4 and multi-layer MXene appeared at 2θ=27.1° and 36.1° as shown in Fig. 4b & c and well matched with the JCPDS cards of 087–1526 and 032–1383. The other binary combinations of the nanocomposites (Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 and Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4) also exhibited similar diffractograms where the peaks of C-QDs, g-C3N4 and MXene are concealed in the resulting XRD pattern (Fig. S3).

The optical properties of the synthesised nanocomposites were analysed by the diffuse reflectance UV–Vis (DR-UV–vis) spectral and photoluminescence analyses (Fig. 5 ). The pristine g-C3N4 exhibits the strong and broad absorption band (λmax) at 385 nm corresponds to the delocalized conjugated system of triazine ring and the multi-layered Ti3C2 was unable to demonstrate any significant absorption from 200 to 800 nm due to the metallic nature and black features of Ti3C2 as evidenced from (Fig. 5a) [49–51]. However, the ternary Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 showed significant hypochromic shift at the 355 and 378 nm due to the heterojunctions resulted from ternary nanocomposites. In addition, visible light absorption of Bi2O2CO3 was red shifted from 380 nm to 440 nm when Bi2O2CO3 was modified with g-C3N4 (Fig. 5a) since the presence of g-C3N4 shifts the absorption band edge of the resulting Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Moreover, the spectral response in visible light region resulted by the quantum confinement effect ascribed from self-decorated C-QDs [52]. The UV-DRS spectra of the individual binary nanocomposite were compared with those of pristine Bi2O2CO3 and the ternary Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 /g-C3N4 nanocomposites to critically evaluate the optical properties of the nanocatalysts as shown in Fig. S4. The results revealed that the band edges of the binary Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4 nanocomposite were similar to those of the ternary Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 /g-C3N4. However, the presence of Ti3C2 in the ternary nanocomposite structure caused a shift in the band edges from 465 nm to 440 nm. The observed shift in the visible light response of ternary nanocomposites enhances the possibilities of generation of electronic charge carriers (e− and h+ pair) and delayed recombination are expected based on the DR-UV–vis spectral analysis, resulting to the enhanced quantum efficiency in the mineralisation processes. Furthermore, the optical bandgap of the nanocomposites were calculated by the linear extrapolation to the photon energy (eV) from the Tauc plot (αhv)2.. The bandgap of pristine Ti3C2, g-C3N4, pristine Bi2O2CO3 and ternary Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 were estimated to be 2.25, 2.7, 3.45 and 2.6 eV as shown in Fig. 5b. The formation of heterostructure with g-C3N4 and Ti3C2 remarkably increased the optical bandgap towards the visible light region. The incorporation of g-C3N4 and MXene extended the adsorption edge of Bi2O2CO3 to the visible light region due to the formation of mid energy level structures which is conducive to the sonophotocatalytic degradation [53–55].

The separation of sonophoto-generated charge carriers in the designed ternary nanocomposites was analysed using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Fig. 5c). The separation efficiency of charge carriers is inversely proportional to the relative intensity of PL spectra [56]. Fig. 5c presents the PL spectra of pristine g-C3N4, BOC, BGC, BMC and BTG nanocomposites excited at 305 nm, confirming a substantial recombination effect in pristine g-C3N4 (λ∼355 nm) due to the lack of a charge migration architecture and wide visible light absorption. In contrast, the binary nanocomposites such as Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4, and Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 exhibited a reduced decay of charges to certain extent (I = 2391 and 3236) attributed to the presence of g-C3N4 and Ti3C2 band levels, which facilitates potential charge migration. Interestingly, the ternary C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4nanocomposite demonstrated a superior enhancement in separation efficiency (21-fold) along with a bathochromic shift (364 nm) resulting from the presence of the charge migration architecture (g-C3N4/Ti3C2) when compared with binary Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4. This result reveals that the successful formation electronic charge transfer between Bi2O2CO3 and g-C3N4 through Ti3C2 migration architecture effectively enables the separation of electrons and holes, extending their lifetime and thereby enhancing the radical generation during sonophotocatalytic elimination of BPA/BPF.

The presence of various bonding and functional groups was further confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 5d. The pristine MXene exhibited stretching vibration modes at 3438 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, 1373 cm−1, 1095 cm−1,and 624 cm−1 could be ascribed to the −O-H, −C = O, −O-H, −C-F, and −Ti-O bonds [57–59]. Similarly, pristine g-C3N4 demonstrated vibrational modes of −N-H, –OH, −C-N, −C = O, and −C = H bonds and triazine cycles [60,61]. The pure Bi2O2CO3 exhibited vibrational modes of −Bi-O, Bi-O-Bi, and carbonate functional groups, which are consistent with previous reports [62–64]. Upon analysing the ternary nanocomposites the characteristic modes of MXene and g-C3N4 were merged with vibrational modes of Bi2O2CO3 vibrational frequencies (Fig. 5d).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to validate the elemental composition and oxidation states of the as-prepared C-QDs@ Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. The characteristic peaks belonging to the Bi, O, C, N, and Ti were identified from the XPS analysis and further to understand the modifications transpired in the Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 and the pristine Bi2O2CO3, the corresponding XPS data was compared (Fig. 6 a). The counterparts of Ti3C2 and g-C3N4 in BTG is confirmed by the presence of N 1 s and Ti 2p. The high-resolution XPS scan was carried out to confirm the existing elements of pristine Bi2O2CO3 and Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Fig. 6b illustrates the Bi 4f orbital states scan and the binding energies observed at 162.3 eV and 156.8 eV corresponds to the orbitals of 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 indicating that Bi is existing in its + 3 oxidation state [65,66]. Moreover, the binding energy distance between the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 orbitals was determined to be 5.5 eV. In comparison, for the binary nanocomposite (Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4), the distance was measured as 5.3 eV. This variation in electronic orbital distance arises from the altered band structure between the ternary and binary nanocomposites (Fig. S5a). The elemental states of C 1 s in BTG exhibit peaks at binding energies of 286.8 eV and 282.7 eV, demonstrating the presence of C-O and C = O groups which confirms the validity of –CO3 2- functional group in the resulting Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 ternary nanocomposites [67]. Furthermore, a shoulder peak was observed in the C 1 s spectra at 283.9 eV, corresponds to the g-C3N4 (Fig. 6c). Moreover, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra also featured the presence of sp2 C = C / sp3 C-C at 284.3 eV and C-O bond at 284.9 eV resulted from the C-QDs and g-C3N4 (insert of Fig. 6c). The N 1 s orbital scans can be subdivided into three binding energy peaks at 399.1 eV, 397.8 eV, and 396.7 eV, which are consistent to the C-NH, N-CH3, and bridging N atoms (−C-N = C) congruently [68–70]. Besides, the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 6d) depicts the heterocycle charging effects (π-excitation) at 402.6 eV [71]. The Ti 2p scans of the ternary nanocomposite are displayed in Fig. 6e. The binding energies of Ti in MXene are positioned at 462.6 eV and 456.8 eV which are ascribed to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbital levels [72,73]. The constituent peaks corresponding to Ti-C (2p3/2) and C-Ti-O were observed at binding energies of 456.7 eV and 457.4 eV, respectively, characteristic bonds of titanium carbide [74]. Additionally, the peak at 458.9 eV was attributed to Ti-O bonds, indicating surface oxidation of MXene. The deconvoluted 2p1/2 peaks of the Ti 2p orbital also revealed Ti-C and Ti-O bonds, measured at 462.3 eV and 463.1 eV, respectively. The presence of Ti-O bonds confirms surface oxidation in MXene and suggests the presence of terminal groups. The high-resolution scan of the O 1 s orbital determined three binding energies: 531.05 eV, 530.2 eV, and 529.4 eV corresponding to the predominant Bi-O bonds, surface hydroxyl groups, and carbonate bonds [45,75]. Concurrently, the binding energies of 530.2 eV and 529.4 eV are attributed to lattice oxygen species in Bi2O2CO3 and are consistent with Bi3+ oxidation states. In addition, the high resolution XPS scans of BTG were compared with those of Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4, used as the control sample. As expected, the Bi, O, C, and N orbitals exhibited shifts in binding energy, attributed to the intrinsic charge transfer from Bi2O2CO3 to g-C3N4, as illustrated in Fig. S5.

The surface charges of the synthesized C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were determined using zeta potential studies to establish the optimal pH conditions for maximizing the catalytic decomposition of BPA under multi-frequency assisted sonophotocatalysis. As depicted in Fig. S6a, the zeta potential of the synthesized C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites resulted that its surface is positively charged (+14.65 mV) whereas the targeted contaminant BPA is negatively charged under natural aquatic conditions [76,77]. Additionally, the zero-point charge (pHzpc) of the synthesized nanocomposites was evaluated across the pH range of 2 to 12 (Fig. S6b). The results revealed that the pHzpc of the BTG nanocomposites is slightly acidic (6.32) which can be attributed to the cationic nature of Bi3+ in Bi2O2CO3. These results confirm that the catalyst and contaminant possess opposite charges facilitating electrostatic interactions for the better adsorption. Since BPA leaching typically occurs in neutral waters, a neutral pH was selected for subsequent experiments to optimize catalytic efficiency and to ensure practical adaptability in real-wastewater treatment.

The sonophotocatalytic decomposition of BPA was carried out with blue LED light and the selective ultrasound irradiation to evaluate the sonophotocatalytic efficiency of the as-prepared C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Prior to the simultaneous irradiation, a fresh BPA solution comprising of 20 ppm concentration was prepared and a determined quantity of catalyst was introduced to the solution. The heterogeneous mixture wasthen stirred for 45 min to establish adsorption/desorption equilibrium at the catalyst surfaces. At first, the baseline condition of 20 kHz sonophotocatalysis was conductedto optimize the catalyst dosage as depicted in Fig. 7 a. The catalyst dosage was varied from 0.25 g/L to 2.5 g/L to reconnoitre the optimized condition with maximum efficacy. The maximum efficiency of sonophotocatalytic degradation of BPA was accomplished as 1 g/L of the nanocomposites dosage and the same concentration was maintained in all the subsequent experiments. In addition, the sonophotocatalytic BPA degradation experiments were carried with the constant illumination of visible light (Blue LED (λ = 457 nm)) along with various ultrasonic frequencies like mono (20, 40 and 80 kHz), dual (20 + 40, 40 + 80 and 80 + 20 kHz), and triple frequencies (20 + 40 + 80 kHz). Fig. 7b demonstrates the mono-frequency assisted sonophotocatalytic degradation resulted with the fixed concentration of BPA and the corresponding degradation percentage could be observed as 61.1, 59.3, and 63.8% for 20, 40, and 80 kHz respectively. When the combination of dual frequencies of 20 and 40 kHz were employed in the sonophotocatalytic BPA degradation, the efficiency increased to 70.5%. Besides, the declined BPA decomposition resulted when the slurry was irradiated with 20 + 80 kHz frequencies which tends to attain 32.7% of degradation in 90 min. The observed decrease in the percentage of BPA degradation was due to the negative interference of ultrasound waves leading to the lower cavitational events in the sonophotocatalytic microenvironment [78]. The degradation percentage of BPA in the presence of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 along with the combinational irradiation of light and 20 + 40 + 80 kHz ultrasound energy leading to achieve 80.2% within 90 min (Fig. 7 and Fig. S7). The enhanced sonophotocatalytic efficacy is related to the positive interference and the increased cavitational events produced at the boundaries of each frequency region. Furthermore, the positive interference of ultrasonic frequencies (20 + 40 + 80 kHz) enhances the production of hydroxyl radicals when compared to the mono and dual frequencies applied in the sonophotocatalytic degradation of BPA. By fitting the degradation data into the first-order kinetics equation, the apparent sonophotocatalytic rate constant k can be determined (linear regression coefficients (r2)values are ≈ 0.99 in all the experiments) as shown in Fig. 7c. Under the conditions of triple frequency assisted sonophotocatalysis, the rate of BPA decomposition by C-QDs@ Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4nanocomposites was calculated to be 0.0180 min−1, which is four times higher than 20 + 80 kHz system and 1.7 times greater than the mono frequency system (20 kHz). Fig. 7d shows the overall comparison of the degrees of BPA degradation under various mono and multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic environments.

As demonstrated in Fig. 7e and f, the ability to degrade BPA was assessed under various nanocatalyst systems for the comparison of degradation efficiency. The optimized triple-frequency assisted sonophotocatalytic BPA degradation was conducted with pristine g-C3N4, Ti3C2 MXene, and Bi2O2CO3. Furthermore, binary combinations including Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4, Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2, and commercially available TiO2 (P25) were also utilized in the comparative investigations. Notably, BPA was found to be resistant under the 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonophotolysis after 90 min of irradiation. After 90 min of irradiation along with various catalytic systems, the multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic decomposition of BPA occurred in the following order: Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 > Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4 > Bi2O2CO3 > TiO2 > Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 > g-C3N4 > Ti3C2 > sonophotolysis. By fitting the degradation data into first-order kinetics, the apparent rate constants of each sonophotocatalyst system could be determined. The rate of BPA decomposition by C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 is 3.7-fold higher than that of commercial P25 (Fig. 7e) nonetheless, the Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 binary nanocomposite expressed the negative synergy when compared to the pristine Bi2O2CO3. Since, the absence of heterojunction between Ti3C2 and Bi2O2CO3 the electronic charge carriers generated from Bi2O2CO3 may be restricted and may recombine quickly. The degradation rate of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 is 4.3-fold higher than that of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 binary nanocomposite. Fig. 7f illustrates the total degradation efficiency of all catalyst systems taken for the investigation compared with sonophotolysis process. On the other hand, Energy consumption is a critical factor in evaluating and scaling up environmental remediation processes particularly in the current generation of sustainable technologies. Therefore, obtained results were subjected to the energy and cost analysis to determine the cost-to-degrade (CTD) for BPA as presented in Table 1 . A comprehensive analysis of energy and cost across the various operational modes in this study indicates that the synthesized BTG nanocomposites exhibit a promising CTD value for BPA degradation (Table 1).

In order to quantitatively evaluate the enhancement of BPA decomposition by multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis system, the synergy percentage was calculated according to their first order kinetics rate. The synergy % was calculated using the Eq. (1).

(i) In the case of multi-frequency (20 + 40 + 80 kHz) system, we observed 52% synergism when compared to the corresponding individual processes resulting from equation (1). The augmented generation of cavitational events at the frequency interferences enhances the number of •OH radicals generation in the triple frequency assisted sonophotocatalysis. The rate of photocatalytic degradation of BPA employing BTG catalyst exhibited only 0.0025 min−1, whereas 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonocatalysis exhibited the BPA degradation rate of 0.0060 min−1 in 90 min. Whereas the combined sonophotocatalysis enhances the surface cleaning with ultrasonic waves in parallel with sonophotocatalysis and improved mass transfer could be achieved, thus leads to demonstrate 52% synergistic effect. The overall comparison of BPA degradation under the various AOPs was demonstrated in Fig. 8 a.

(ii) In the case of dual-frequency sonophotocatalytic modes, we observed antagonistic effect on BPA degradation by 20 + 80 kHz system. However, all of the dual-frequency sonophotocatalytic systems exhibited the antagonistic behaviour. The destructive interference of ultrasound energy within the sonophotoenvironment demonstrates the antagonistic implications. In other words, destructive interference can result from out-of-phase compression and rarefaction of one frequency which nullifies with other frequency. The destructive interference causes reduced cavitational events and nucleation, thereby lowering sonophotocatalytic efficiency. Fig. 8b shows the multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis based synergistic effect attained for the various combination of ultrasound. Particularly, the antagonistic effect of −3.95 (synergy index) for the 20 + 80 kHz system resulted in low BPA removal. On the other hand, the BPA degradation was higher (20 + 40 kHz) in comparison with mono-frequency investigations, even though the other systems also showed antagonistic effects in terms of synergy index (20 + 80 kHz and 40 + 80 kHz).

(iii) The heterojunction-driven synergy was determined as shown in Fig. 8c. Eq. (2) was applied to calculate the synergy in binary nanocomposites assisted BPA degradation. The first-order rate equation yields the rate for different sonophotocatalysts of pristine Bi2O2CO3, g-C3N4, and MXene as 0.0818, 0.00106, and 0.0018 respectively. The synergy index was calculated as 0.37 after subtracting the sonophotolysis results. Identically, the significance of Z-scheme-like heterojunction was determined for the binary nanocomposite by evaluating their synergy index. For the sonophotocatalytic systems of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2 and Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4, the synergy index values were −1.14 and −0.19 due to absence of suitable charge migration architecture. The longer lifetime of the charge carriers was accountable for the increased sonophotocatalytic rate in terms of BPA elimination in the presence of C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites.

Subsequently, the augmented hydroxyl radical generation was evidenced by the terephthalic acid dosimetry method using fluorescence analysis (FL) as illustrated in Fig. 8d. The intensity of FL implies the formation of fluorescent active 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) in aqueous solution. The higher FL intensity indicates the greater formation of OH-substituted product (HTA) [79–81]. The investigation was conducted to quantify and compare the OH• radicals production during multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis and sonophotolysis processes. Consequently, the sonophotolysis (20 + 40 + 80 kHz) yielded low intensified FL whereas 16 times enhanced FL intensity was resulted for sonophotocatalysis under the same experimental conditions which results from the enhanced OH• radical production. Furthermore, the FL dosimetry suggests that the OH● radicals generated by the multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis employed with C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were 2.4-fold higher than the pristine Bi2O2CO3, which also aligns with the interest in kinetic rate and synergy calculations as discussed earlier. The increased OH• radical production suggested that the sonophotocatalytically generated charge carriers were well separated through the Z-scheme. The plausible mechanism of multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis was discussed in Section 3.5.

Bisphenol F (BPF) is an organic chemical that possesses an analogous structure of BPA. Many studies have proven that the BPF as the next-generation life-threatening endocrine disruptor due to its wide application in epoxy resins and as an additive in plastic manufacturing [85,86]. BPF can be generated through the demethylation of BPA and is regarded as a more severe EDC due to its tendency to disrupt much signalling pathways. Recognizing this, we attempted to degrade BPF with the optimized conditions of the nanocomposites and multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic systems (20 + 40 + 80 kHz). Analogues to BPA experiments and the HPLC technique were used to assess the degree of BPF elimination (20 ppm). Remarkably, we removed 80% of BPF using combined light and ultrasonic irradiation at 20 + 40 + 80 kHz in 90 min using Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (Fig. S8). Due to the fact BPA and BPF can coexist in an aqueous environment, we investigated the first-time simultaneous elimination of BPF-BPA EDCs from each 20-ppm concentration under our optimal experimental conditions. Surprisingly, the proposed nanocomposites and optimized experimental conditions of multi-frequency sonophotocatalysis can degrade both BPA and BPF in a coexisting environment. Nonetheless, we observed a slight enhancement in BPA degradation, which is due to BPA molecules being effectively adsorbed at the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 surfaces. As a result, multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic processes destroyed up to 66% and 87% of the BPF and BPA respectively (Fig. 9 ). The enhancement in BPA elimination is attributed to higher adsorption, which is augmented by the + I inductive effect of methyl groups, and also reduced mobility in the aqueous environment. Furthermore, this simultaneous removal experiment is a pilot attempt to eliminate BPA-BPF in the technically advanced hybrid AOP approach. In the future, we will conduct intensive experiments using the various concentrations, combinations, and conditions to acquire the comprehensive mineralisation of BPA-BPF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation intermediates and pathway of sonophotocatalysis
Ecological toxicity estimation of identified BPA degradation intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: Nevertheless, the multi-frequency assisted sonophotocatalytic process effectively degraded the BPA as discussed in Section 3.1 to 3.4, It is imperative to assess the toxicity of identified intermediates to establish a clean ecological balance between aquatic environments. Therefore, the identified intermediates were subjected to Toxicity analysis and compared with BPA using Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T) which functions based on the Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) [94]. The indexes of toxicity evaluation were studied such as LC50 (Semi-lethal concentration) and IGC50 (Growth Inhibition Concentration). The higher toxicity level of intermediates can be related with lower value of LC50 and IGC50. The identified intermediates and their indexes were evaluated against Fathead minnow, T. pyriformis and Daphnia magna. As illustrated in Fig. 11 , all intermediates exhibited relatively lower toxicity compared to the parent BPA. The predicted LC50 toxicity values for Fathead minnow was significantly higher for all intermediates than for BPA, which signifies the severity of BPA's impact on aquatic ecosystems (Fig. 11a). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 11b and c, the predicted toxicity values of IGC50 and LC50 for T. Pyriformis and Daphnia Magna were notably higher than those of BPA. Moreover, this QSAR study also revealed that certain intermediates, such as P1, P2, P4, P8, and P11 demonstrated slightly lower values likely to BPA. The observation indicates some residual toxicity due to the Bisbenzene structure, the degradation pathway effectively reduced the overall toxicity. The sequential mineralization of intermediates through multi-frequency-assisted sonophotocatalysis led to smaller, ring-opened by-products. The smaller intermediates exhibited significantly reduced toxicity towards T. pyriformis and D. magna, clearly demonstrating that the intermediates identified during BPA degradation were less harmful.

The stability and recurrent use of a heterogeneous catalyst are significant variables in wastewater treatment studies. In this context, the reusability of synthesized nanocomposites under optimized multi-frequency conditions was evaluated against the BPA degradation. As shown in Fig. 12 a, the multi-frequency-assisted sonophotocatalytic degradation demonstrated superior catalytic efficiency with minimal loss in the third consecutive cycle (∼8%). Moreover, the kinetic rate for the third cycle was calculated to be 0.0142 min−1 when compared to 0.01805 min−1 and 0.018037 min−1 for the first and second cycles. The recovered nanocomposite was analysed using XRD and DR UV–Vis techniques to assess the stability of the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as shown in Fig. 12b and Fig. 12c. The XRD patterns of fresh and recycled BTG nanocomposites revealed almost no changes even after the catalytic degradation of BPA. However, upon careful analysis a slight red shift at higher diffraction angles and peak broadening were noticed which indicates an increase in particle size due to batch treatment with ultrasound exposure. It was understood from Fig. 12b, no new diffraction peaks or amorphous characteristics (indicative of phase changes) were observed. Consequently, the synthesized nanocomposites maintained stable crystallinity and phase integrity even after three consecutive cycles of 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonophotocatalytic degradation of BPA. The DR UV–Vis spectral analysis of fresh and recycled nanocomposites provided significant insights related to the observed loss of catalytic efficiency during the third cycle of BPA degradation under optimized multi-frequency conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 12c, the spectra of fresh and recycled nanocomposites (1st and 2nd cycles) exhibited similar spectral response with a slight change in absorbance likely due to the adsorption of water molecules on the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 surface. However, the DR UV–Vis spectra of the nanocomposites after the third cycle displayed a significant hypochromic shift in the 200–320 nm region accompanied by slight deformation of the band shape at 440 nm (Fig. 12c). The observed changes indicate that the optical characteristics of the nanocomposites were altered after the third cycle, contributing to the observed 8% reduction in BPA degradation efficiency (Fig. 12a).

Carbon-based quantum dots (C-QDs) are typically synthesized using a variety of carbon precursors as carbon source, including ascorbic acid, citric acid, sucrose, and glucose. In our study, the application of ultrasound to the nanocomposite precursors (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and Na2CO3) enhances dispersion and facilitates interactions particularly involving carbonate ions. The ultrasound irradiation initiates the nucleation whereas the ultrasound alone unable to complete the formation of C- QDs. The subsequent hydrothermal treatment under high pressure and temperature drives the carbonization of carbonate ions on the surfaces of Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Additionally, ultrasound aids in disaggregating precursor clusters, improving uniformity and synthetic efficiency as evidenced from HR-TEM micrographs (Fig. 2-3 and Fig. S1b − c). The C-QDs are prominently observed on the surfaces of Bi2O2CO3 indicating that their formation is driven by carbonate ions and substantially enhanced by the ultrasound-assisted hydrothermal approach. While various studies have described similar mechanisms for C-QD synthesis, the decoration of C-QDs on Bi2O2CO3​ surfaces remains a relatively unexplored area, offering potential for further investigation [46–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible degradation mechanism of multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic mineralization of BPA
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis (Fig. 10; Table S2) and related literature reports [39,95–97], in connection with the results of dosimetry analysis and the bandgap derived from Tauc plot, a possible mechanism for BPA degradation by C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite under multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic environments could be illustrated in Fig. 13 . g-C3N4 is a two-dimensional metal-free polymeric nanomaterial with a narrow bandgap (2.7 eV), facilitating it to act as a p-type semiconductor. Bi2O2CO3 processing n-type semiconductor characteristics due to its wide bandgap (3.4 eV), stable electronic properties and active charge carrier generation. On the other hand, Ti3C2 is an emerging 2D nanomaterial with a wide range of surface and functional attributes, including hydrophobicity, surface groups, charge transfer, and Schottky junction characteristics. The as-prepared C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites establishes the Z-scheme sonophotoelectron migration which is demonstrated in Fig. 13. When multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic conditions are triggered, light energy originates from enhanced sonoluminescence, and photons from the LED bulbs (λ max = 457 nm) induces electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of nanocomposites [8,98]. The VB and CB energies of g-C3N4 are +1.58 and −1.12 eV, while those of Bi2O2CO3 are + 3.73 and + 0.35 eV, respectively [39,99]. The bandgap energies for g-C3N4 and Bi2O2CO3 are 2.7 and 3.4 eV, respectively. The CB level of g-C3N4 is more negative than that of Bi2O2CO3, and as a result, sonophoto-excited electrons (e−) from Bi2O2CO3 can be migrated to the VB of g-C3N4 via the electron mediator role demonstrated by Ti3C2 MXene [100,101]. Besides, migrating electrons can be energized to the g-C3N4 fermi level. The CB of g-C3N4 (−1.12 eV vs. NHE) is more negative than the potential of O2/O2 • − (−0.33 eV), so it will undergo redox reactions with molecular oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). As a result, excited electrons can be efficiently separated from recombination and effectively reacted to produce the ROS, as evidenced by photoluminescence investigations. (Fig. 5c) . The lifetime of charge carriers (e− and h+) is further extended by Ti3C2 MXene's electron sink characteristics, and presence of surface decorated C-QDs which also improve the separation of holes and electrons. The contribution of active functional groups in MXene surfaces will likely promote BPA adsorption on the nanocomposites surface. In addition, the VB energy of Bi2O2CO3 is higher than ψ (H2O/HO•, +2.72 eV), which indicated the generation of HO• radicals via the sonophotooxidation of water molecules by the holes. These enhanced separations of charge carriers will intensify the generation of ROS in the aquatic environment, which can attack the BPA molecules via various chemical pathways such as oxidation, decarboxylation, demethylation, ring rupture, ring opening reactions, and more. The effective attack of BPA molecules can breakdown the large molecules into small fragments and finally causes the mineralization (Fig. S9). Based on the experimental investigations, it can be understood that the increased production of cavitational bubbles from the multi-frequency reactor and effective charge separation structure of C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite is the sole reason for the augmented degradation efficiency. Table 2 summarizes the catalytic degradation of C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites towards BPA decomposition. It was clear that the simultaneous irradiation of multi-low frequency (20 + 40 + 80 kHz) assisted sonophotocatalytic degradation of BPA in presence of C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposites demonstrates better catalytic elimination of BPA as compared to various works reported in the literature (Table 2).

In this work, C-QDs precisely self-decorated on novel Z-scheme, MXene derived ternary nanocomposite was synthesised for the degradation of BPA in multi-frequency sonophotocatalytic conditions are reported for the first time. The Bi2O2CO3 is supported with g-C3N4 and Ti3C2 MXene using simple hydrothermal treatment to synthesize the ternary nanocomposites. The presence of small C-QDs was evidenced from HR-TEM micrographs, and the size of the dots was in the range of 6 to 7 nm. Furthermore, the optical properties and phase purity of synthesized nanocomposite were characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy and XRD analyses, while XPS demonstrated that Bi exists in the +3 oxidation state. In addition, vibrational modes of various bonds in nanocomposites were confirmed by FTIR analysis.

The sonophotocatalytic characteristics of the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite was investigated by the degradation of BPA as a model contaminant under various operational conditions (20 kHz, 40 kHz, and 80 kHz). Among the synthesized nanocomposites and experimental conditions, the C-QDs@Bi2O2CO3/Ti3C2/g-C3N4 exhibited the highest catalytic efficiency (80% within 90 min) with a 0.0180 min−1 rate under 20 + 40 + 80 kHz sonophotocatalysis. The enhanced degradation efficiency of ternary nanocomposite under multi-frequency sonophotoconditions is attributed to effective charge carrier’s separation, increased cavitational events produced by the multi-frequency reactor and improved mass transfer (52% synergism). Moreover, we attempted to degrade BPA and BPF in simultaneous removal under the optimized sonophotocatalytic conditions. Finally, the degradation pathway was proposed based on the HRMS/QTof analysis. The present investigation demonstrates that MXene-supported nanocomposites decorated with C-QDs can effectively degrade EDCs such as BPA and BPF under innovative multi-frequency ultrasound assisted sonophotocatalysis.
